vpnNote: You will need to be on Trifork's network or connected via VPN to access the linked pages.

phases.title: Before contract signing
phases.intro: Work through these decisions to identify the documents and agreements you need before signing.

steps.question: What types of personal data does the project process?
steps.context: "Processing" means anything done to or with personal data — collection, storage, use, disclosure, erasure.

options.label: Anonymous / public
options.hint: Publicly available info, fully anonymised data, synthetic / dummy data.

options.label: Non-sensitive personal data
options.hint: Name, address, email, phone, age, education, IP address.

options.label: Sensitive personal data
options.hint: CPR, health, biometrics, salary / finance, HR cases, criminal records.

steps.question: What customer data or requirements are in scope?
steps.context: Customer criticality covers the customer's data sensitivity, NDA / IP requirements, and any security measures the engagement demands.

options.label: No customer sensitive data or requirements
Internal Duckwise project
options.hint: Internal docs, code, processes, templates, policies.

options.label: Customer / project without NDA
options.hint: Customer code, designs, requirements, anything under contract.

options.label: Secrets and NDA
options.hint: IP, business secrets, passwords, tokens, certificates, vulnerability details, production datasets. Anything under NDA.

steps.question: Is the project critical to society?
steps.context: Society-critical means the client operates in regulated or essential sectors such as finance, healthcare, public infrastructure, or government. If a failure or compromise would affect that sector beyond the client itself, pick yes.

steps.question: How much incorrect data can Duckwise tolerate on this project?
steps.context: An indication of the degree of incorrect data that can be tolerated. Pick the option that best matches the project.

options.label: Low requirement — minimal impact, easy to remediate
Moderate requirement — somewhat affected, easy to detect and remediate
High requirement — inaccuracies or omissions hard to tolerate
Absolute requirement — no inaccuracies or omissions tolerated

steps.question: How long can Duckwise function without the asset before it becomes critical or unacceptable?
steps.context: Pick the maximum tolerable downtime for the project.

steps.question: Will the project use any AI tools?
steps.context: If AI tools are used the client must sign the Aftale om brug af hjælpeværktøjer (Helper tools agreement). The specific tool names go in the agreement itself, not here.

steps.question: Who is the data controller for this project?
steps.context: Controller decides why and how personal data is used. Processor handles the data on the controller's instructions. Sub-processor is a vendor used by a processor (no matter how far down the chain, the term stays "sub-processor"). Example A: Client controller → Trifork processor → Duckwise sub-processor → Company X sub-processor.

steps.question: Is a Data Processor Agreement (DPA) needed?
steps.context: A DPA is needed between every pair of parties that exchanges personal data. The main DPA (controller ↔ processor) must list all sub-processors downstream.

phases.title: Before project start
phases.intro: Confirm tool levels, handbook reading, and consents before the team begins work.

steps.question: Have all team members signed the annual cycle signoff?
steps.context: Section 3 "Team member signoff" on the DK-AAR/CPH-DUCKS Annual cycle BU page must be signed by every team member before they touch the project.

steps.question: Are all tools the team will use listed on the Duckwise systems Level 1/2/3 lists?
steps.context: Each system must be on the correct level, with the level of data processing allowed matching the project's tasks. Client-provided accounts (non-duckwise.dk/trifork.com emails) go on Level 3.

steps.question: Have all team members read the handbooks for the tools being used?
steps.context: Only some tools have an employee handbook. Where one is listed on the Duckwise systems page, every team member must read and understand it before using that tool.

steps.question: Will the project process personal data that requires consent?
steps.context: If personal data is processed, consent must be in place before processing starts. The data controller obtains and stores consent. Consent can be digital but must be demonstrable in writing.

phases.title: During the project
phases.intro: Once a month, check whether anything has changed that would require updating Article 30.

steps.question: Has anything changed this month that affects Article 30?
steps.context: New tools, new data types, new sub-processors, or scope changes can require amending Article 30.

phases.title: At project finish
phases.intro: Close out Article 30 and verify deletion obligations defined in consents and DPAs are met.

steps.question: Did anything change at project end that affects Article 30?
steps.context: Final scope or data movement may require a last amendment before marking the project completed.

steps.question: Has all data that must be deleted actually been deleted?
steps.context: Consents and DPAs define what must be deleted and when. Verify deletion is complete before marking the project closed.

actions.title: Start a new row in Article 30
actions.note: Open the Article 30 register and add a new row for this project. Fill in the columns up to project classification. Use the Copy classification details button above to paste the answers into the "Which type of data is/will be processed?" column.

actions.title: Contact your SDP agent before proceeding. The project has elevated-risk indicators.
actions.note: Any of these triggers SDP-agent involvement before contract signing: sensitive personal data, NDA / business secrets, security-cleared personnel, society-critical sector, absolute integrity requirement, or sub-1-day availability.

actions.title: Have the client sign the Aftale om brug af hjælpeværktøjer (Helper tools agreement)
actions.note: Fill in the tools list, get client signature, upload to Article 30.

actions.title: Create the Data Processor Agreement using the DBA Trifork template
actions.note: Make sure the relationship between controller, processor, and any sub-processors is described, including which systems Duckwise uses and whether they process data outside the EU or outside the US data privacy framework.

actions.title: Contact your SDP agent. The client is unwilling to figure as data controller.
actions.note: If a DPA is needed AND the client refuses the controller role, this must be escalated before continuing.

actions.title: Classify the project and fill in the assessment templates
actions.note: Visit the Duckwise Project Categories page and find the template that best matches your project. Download and fill in the relevant templates, then upload them to the appropriate column in Article 30. If no overall description fits, use the next category above (higher risk) as a starting template and add the project under "Custom project categories" on the same page.

actions.title: Sign Section 3 (Team member signoff) on the annual cycle BU page
actions.note: Every team member signs before touching the project.

actions.title: Add or update each tool on the correct Duckwise systems level (1/2/3)
actions.note: Match the level of data processing allowed to the project's tasks.

actions.title: Read the handbooks for every system the team will use
actions.note: If a system has a handbook listed, every team member must read it before use.

actions.title: List any client-licensed accounts on the Duckwise systems Level 3 list
actions.note: Accounts not using duckwise.dk or trifork.com emails belong on Level 3.

actions.title: Draft the consent form
actions.note: Use the client's template if they have one, otherwise the Trifork Consent template. Run the consent validity checklist (clear language, specific purpose, data described, processing described, controller/processor/sub-processors named, systems and locations described, rights described, checkbox driven).

actions.title: Get written client approval of the consent form
actions.note: Client appears as data controller and sender, and takes responsibility for storing consent documentation. If the client refuses this, contact the SDP agent.

actions.title: Complete Phase 2 to update Article 30
actions.note: Material change detected. Walk through Phase 2 (Before project start) again to refresh classifications, tool levels, and signoffs.

actions.title: Update Article 30 and mark the project completed
actions.note: Final close out. Confirm the row is current and mark complete.

actions.title: Verify that data slated for deletion has actually been deleted
actions.note: Cross-check against consents and DPAs to confirm deletion obligations are met.

actions.title: Complete the outstanding deletions before closing the project
actions.note: Deletions defined by consents and DPAs must be done before marking the project complete.
